one_line_takeaway: On the authors' own 2000-sample Windows ransomware telemetry dataset, one-shard SISA unlearning for DQN/DDQN ransomware detectors preserves F1 within 0.05% of the pre-deletion ensemble while cutting unlearning retraining time roughly 5x (from ~113-123s full-SISA retrain down to ~22-24s), though no formal verification that the deleted data's influence was actually removed is performed.
key_finding: Baseline (single-agent) in-distribution performance: DQN mean F1 = 0.9920 +/- 0.0045 (worst-fold F1 0.9850), OOF Q-score ROC-AUC = 0.9987, training time 23.14s, inference time 0.0365s; DDQN mean F1 = 0.9925 +/- 0.0025 (worst-fold F1 0.9900), OOF ROC-AUC = 0.9983, training time 25.13s, inference time 0.0457s. DDQN's OOF confusion matrix: 99.3% of benign and 99.2% of ransomware samples correctly classified, with FPR 0.7% and FNR 0.8%. Under the SISA ensemble (M=5 shards, majority vote), pre-unlearning F1 was 0.9787 (DQN) and 0.9806 (DDQN); after one-shard fast-unlearning (5% of one shard deleted, only that shard retrained), F1 was 0.9782 (DQN) and 0.9806 (DDQN), giving utility drops (delta-F1) of 0.0005 (DQN, i.e., ~0.05% absolute) and 0.0000 (DDQN). Computational cost: full SISA training (5 shards) took 113.30s (DQN) / 123.21s (DDQN), roughly 5x the single-agent baseline (23.14s / 25.13s), whereas one-shard unlearning retraining took only 22.40s (DQN) / 23.98s (DDQN) -- near-baseline cost, confirming the framework's central claim that shard-isolated retraining avoids the ~5x cost of full SISA retraining while incurring negligible (<=0.05%) F1 degradation.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (Sections 3-7, distinct from the Related Work in Section 2) is an empirical evaluation framework that integrates SISA (Sharded, Isolated, Sliced, and Aggregated) machine unlearning with value-based deep reinforcement learning (DQN and DDQN) for Windows behavioral ransomware detection. The authors train shard-level RL agents (M=5 disjoint shards, majority-vote aggregation), then simulate a privacy-driven deletion request by removing 5% of samples from a single shard and retraining only that shard ('one-shard fast-unlearning'). They measure the resulting change in F1-score (utility preservation) and wall-clock retraining cost against a full-SISA baseline and a single-agent (non-sharded) baseline, using 5-fold stratified cross-validation and a Q-score margin (Q(X,1) - Q(X,0)) for ROC-AUC analysis of RL detection confidence. The paper explicitly frames its novelty as being the first systematic study of SISA-based unlearning applied specifically to RL-based ransomware detection (Section 2.4, 'Gap Addressed by This Study'), as opposed to prior SISA work which the authors state was validated primarily in supervised-learning settings (cited to refs [15,17,18], not their own work).
threat_model: This is not an adversarial/attacker threat model paper. The 'threat' addressed is a compliance/data-governance scenario: a data-deletion request (e.g., GDPR 'right to be forgotten') against a deployed ransomware detector, and the operational question of how to efficiently remove a training sample's influence without full retraining while a) preserving ransomware detection efficacy (the actual security function) and b) avoiding the cost of full model retraining. No adversarial forgetting attacks, membership-inference attacks, or model-extraction threats are evaluated in this paper; the authors explicitly note in the Discussion that they perform no formal verification of forgetting (no membership-inference test or oracle-based forgetting test) and list this as future work.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations (Discussion/Conclusion, Section 6-7): (1) evaluation is restricted to single-shard, single-deletion-request scenarios -- no multi-shard or sequential deletion is tested; (2) no formal/verifiable forgetting is performed (no membership-inference attack or oracle-based forgetting test), so the paper demonstrates utility preservation and speed but not that information was actually removed from the retrained shard's model in a provable/certified sense; (3) restricted to value-based RL (DQN/DDQN) because the Q-score margin requires explicit action-value estimates -- policy-gradient/actor-critic methods are left to future work; (4) dataset is a custom, moderately sized 2000-sample corpus (30 ransomware families) rather than a large-scale or adversarially robust benchmark; (5) no evaluation under adversarial conditions or out-of-distribution/evolving ransomware behavior. Additional grounding-level observation: the paper reports both a 'negligible utility degradation' figure of '<=0.05% absolute F1 drop' (abstract) and 'negligible utility degradation... achieves negligible utility degradation (<=0.2% absolute F1)' (contribution bullet in Section 1) -- the abstract's figure (0.05%) matches the Table 4 DQN result exactly, while the 0.2% figure in the intro appears to be a looser/rounded claim not directly traceable to a specific table cell.

datasets_used: Custom behavioral ransomware dataset: 2000 executables (1000 ransomware spanning 30 families 2019-2024, sourced from MalwareBazaar and VirusShare with multi-vendor VirusTotal verification; 1000 benign samples from SnapFiles, PortableApps, GitHub), executed in ANY.RUN Windows 11 sandbox, reduced to a 103-dimensional feature vector
quantitative_results: Baseline DQN: F1 = 0.9920 +/- 0.0045, worst-fold F1 = 0.9850, OOF ROC-AUC (Q-score) = 0.9987, training time 23.14s, inference time 0.0365s
Baseline DDQN: F1 = 0.9925 +/- 0.0025, worst-fold F1 = 0.9900, OOF ROC-AUC (Q-score) = 0.9983, training time 25.13s, inference time 0.0457s
DDQN OOF confusion matrix: 99.3% benign correctly classified, 99.2% ransomware correctly classified, FPR 0.7%, FNR 0.8%
SISA ensemble (M=5 shards) DQN F1 before unlearning = 0.9787, after one-shard unlearning = 0.9782, delta-F1 = 0.0005
SISA ensemble (M=5 shards) DDQN F1 before unlearning = 0.9806, after one-shard unlearning = 0.9806, delta-F1 = 0.0000
Computational cost DQN: baseline train 23.14s, full SISA train (5 shards) 113.30s, one-shard unlearn retrain 22.40s
Computational cost DDQN: baseline train 25.13s, full SISA train (5 shards) 123.21s, one-shard unlearn retrain 23.98s
baselines_compared: DQN vs. DDQN (value-based RL algorithm comparison, identical architecture/hyperparameters, differing only in TD-target computation)
Single-agent (non-sharded) baseline training vs. full SISA training (5 shards) vs. one-shard SISA fast-unlearning retraining
SISA ensemble F1 before vs. after one-shard unlearning (utility preservation comparison)
